explanation: OR outputs 1 whenever at least one input is 1, so 1 OR 1 = 1. Do not confuse OR with XOR: XOR would give 0 here because it outputs 1 only when the inputs differ.
NOR is OR followed by NOT: 1 OR 1 = 1, then inverted gives 0. A NOR gate outputs 1 only when both inputs are 0.
In an RS (SR) flip-flop, S is Set (drives Q to 1) and R is Reset (drives Q to 0). The other options are nonsense words placed to catch careless reading.

R=1 with S=0 asserts the Reset input, so Q goes to 0. Option C is the trap: 'set to zero' misuses the word set, which in flip-flop terminology specifically means driving the output to 1. Note R=1 and S=1 together is the forbidden/invalid state.
This is the standard textbook definition of a DBMS: software that controls creation, maintenance, and use of an organization's databases. True.
The data dictionary is the system catalog: a computer-based directory of metadata (table names, column names, types, constraints, users), not a store of commands or of the user data itself.
SQL stands for Structured Query Language. 'Standard Query Language' is the common trap answer.
Metadata literally means data about data: it describes the structure of the stored data (schemas, field types, lengths) rather than being the data values themselves.

The ANSI/SPARC three-schema architecture has three levels: internal (physical storage), conceptual (logical whole-database structure), and external (individual user views). Separating them is what gives physical and logical data independence.

The DBA and database designer define schemas with the Data Definition Language (DDL) - statements like CREATE TABLE and ALTER TABLE. The trap is option D: DML is Data Manipulation Language (SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE), which operates on data, not on schema definitions.
MS-Access is a desktop relational DBMS used to create and manage databases, so True. Careful with the labels here: True is option B, not A.
MS-Excel is a spreadsheet application, so the statement is True.
A file is defined as a collection of related records/data, so True. Note the label order is reversed on this item: True is option B.

The four common file organization methods taught here are sequential, index-sequential, random (direct), and multi-key. Option A supplies the first two and option B the second two, so only 'D: 1 and 2' covers the full set. Option C is a decoy because it says 'serial' rather than 'sequential'.

Common data file types include master, transaction, and history files (option 1) and table, control, and report files (option 3), so the answer is 'D: 1 and 3'. Option B (large/medium/small) is not a classification of file type at all, which is the giveaway.

A data block is the smallest unit of data that can be transferred to or from secondary storage in a single access; the disk cannot read half a block. Option C is the trap: a block usually holds several records (blocking factor), so it is not one record.
True. A physical write is the actual transfer of a buffer's contents out to the external file/device, as opposed to a logical write, which only moves a record into the program's buffer in memory.

True per the course definition: the blocking routine sits between the file and the program, reading a whole block from disk once and then handing the program one record (component) at a time. Strictly speaking, splitting a block into records is called de-blocking, so the wording is loose, but the intended answer is True.
False. Odd parity sets the parity bit so the total number of 1 bits is ODD; the stem describes EVEN parity. Careful with the labels: False is option A here.

A secondary key is any field other than the primary key that is used as an access key to retrieve records. The trap is reading 'secondary' positionally: it has nothing to do with being the second key or second field in the record.
